coredata.dc:title: SiO2-coated molecularly imprinted sensor based on Si quantum dots for selective detection of catechol in river water
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Yeqing
Huang, Ting
Wang, Suao
Meng, Minjia
Yan, Yongsheng
coredata.dc:description: Selectivity is an important goal to the final analysis of targets. But for silicon quantum dots (Si QDs), a kind of QDs with low-toxic from a wide range of sources, the selective detection is mainly through the special force between Si QDs and targets. Yet it is necessary to find a way to realize the selective detection for more common target analysis. Herein, the molecularly imprinted polymers based on Si QDs (Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs) was successfully prepared for selective detection of catechol (CT). Si QDs were firstly modified with 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), the template molecule CT was effectively contacted with Si QDs after modifications, under the action of cross-linking agent tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs were synthesized through the sol-gel method. Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs displayed a strong fluorescent emission at 447 nm and were quenched in the presence of CT via dynamic quenching. Under optimum conditions, the relative fluorescent intensity of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs was gradually weakened in response to the increased CT concentrations in the range of 0.018–100 µM. A low detection limit of 0.018 µM for CT determination was obtained. This developed strategy was evaluated by detecting CT in the real river water samples. Satisfactory recoveries were obtained in the range of 97.77−101.70%. Moreover, the high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method was conducted as a reference of CT detection, the results powerfully demonstrated the accuracy and feasibility of the method proposed in this work. Thus, the strategy is possibly a promising method for Si QDs to expand more target detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted technology
Silicon quantum dots
Selective detection
Catechol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of CT in water samples
Synthesis and characterization of Si QDs and Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs
Optimization of the detection conditions of Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs
Analytical performance of the Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of Si QDs (a) and Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs (b).
TEM images of Si QDs (a) and Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs (b); SEM image of Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs (c).
FT-IR spectra of Si QDs (a), Si QDs@SiO2@NIPs (b) and Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs (c).
High resolution XPS spectra of Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs. Full range (a), C1s (b), N1s (c), O1s (d), Si2p (e) and XRD pattern of Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs (f).
Fluorescence excitation spectrum (a), fluorescence emission spectrum (b), and UV–vis absorption spectrum of Si QDs (c). (Inset: photographs of Si QDs solutions under visible light and UV lamp).

(a) The fluorescence intensity of Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs in 120 min, (b) the fluorescence intensity of Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs (black line) and Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs exposed to 80 µM CT (red line), (c) the fluorescence intensity ratio (I0/I) changes with 80 µM CT in 120 min.
Fluorescence spectra and the corresponding linearities of the Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs (a, c) and Si QDs@SiO2@NIPs (b, d) under the CT concentration range of 0–100 µM.

(a) The fluorescence intensity ratio (I0/I) changes of Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs with 100 µM targets (blue bars), Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs with 100 μM CT and 100 μM analogs (orange bars), Si QDs@SiO2@NIPs with 100 μM targets (green bars) and Si QDs@SiO2@NIPs with 100 μM CT and 100 μM analogs (pink bars); (b) the molecular structures of CT, BPA, 2, 6-DP, HQ, 2, 4-DP, RS and 2, 4, 6-TP.

Fluorescence intensity ratio (I0/I) of Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs (black bars) and Si QDs@SiO2@NIPs (red bars) exposed to 100 µM CT and 5.0 mM Cu2+, Na+, Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Fe3+, NO3 -, Cl- and SO4 2-.

(a) The absorption spectra of CT (black curve), Si QDs (red curve) and Si QDs with CT (blue curve), fluorescence excitation spectrum (pink curve) and fluorescence emission spectrum (green curve) of Si QDs; (b). The zeta potentials of Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs and CT; (c, d). The fluorescence lifetime curves of Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs and Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs with CT.
Comparisons of CT determination results in river water samples by fluorescence analysis method and HPLC method.
Comparison of different sensors for catechol detection.

head.ce:title: SiO2-coated molecularly imprinted sensor based on Si quantum dots for selective detection of catechol in river water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, Advanced Chemical Engineering Laboratory of Green Materials and Energy of Jiangsu Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, Advanced Chemical Engineering Laboratory of Green Materials and Energy of Jiangsu Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, Advanced Chemical Engineering Laboratory of Green Materials and Energy of Jiangsu Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Selectivity is an important goal to the final analysis of targets. But for silicon quantum dots (Si QDs), a kind of QDs with low-toxic from a wide range of sources, the selective detection is mainly through the special force between Si QDs and targets. Yet it is necessary to find a way to realize the selective detection for more common target analysis. Herein, the molecularly imprinted polymers based on Si QDs (Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs) was successfully prepared for selective detection of catechol (CT). Si QDs were firstly modified with 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), the template molecule CT was effectively contacted with Si QDs after modifications, under the action of cross-linking agent tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs were synthesized through the sol-gel method. Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs displayed a strong fluorescent emission at 447 nm and were quenched in the presence of CT via dynamic quenching. Under optimum conditions, the relative fluorescent intensity of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs was gradually weakened in response to the increased CT concentrations in the range of 0.018–100 µM. A low detection limit of 0.018 µM for CT determination was obtained. This developed strategy was evaluated by detecting CT in the real river water samples. Satisfactory recoveries were obtained in the range of 97.77−101.70%. Moreover, the high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method was conducted as a reference of CT detection, the results powerfully demonstrated the accuracy and feasibility of the method proposed in this work. Thus, the strategy is possibly a promising method for Si QDs to expand more target detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, nanomaterials have played an important role in the design of chemical sensors/biosensors [1–3]. Fluorescent nanomaterials are regarded as a new avenue to be used in designing analysis [4–6]. Quantum dots (QDs), as one of the fluorescent nanomaterials, are widely used in the field of sensing for the high-sensitivity determination of some analytes [7]. Silicon quantum dots (Si QDs) are preferred among the QDs owing to prominent advantages, such as abundant and easy availability of silicon sources, favorable biocompatibility [8,9], low toxicity [10–12] and surface modifiable [13,14]. Moreover, the storage of silicon on the earth ranks second [10], which provides a rich source for low-cost and large-scale synthesis of Si QDs, so its research is also of great significance. For example, Zhou et al. [15] used COOH functionalized Si QDs to be chelated with Eu(NO3)3 to form SiQDs-Eu3+, then achieved the selective detection of tetracycline, Zinc, and Cadmium based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer; Phan et al. [16] used Cr(VI) to effectively bind with the surfaces of Si QDs via coordinate covalent bonds and achieved the selective detection of Cr(VI); Liu et al. [17] used Si QDs to form a complex with 1,4-benzoquinone, an oxidation product of hydroquinone (HQ), then realized selective detection of HQ. However, these targets all have special effects with Si QDs (such as chelation, coordination, complexation, etc.) or can produce fluorescence resonance energy transfer with Si QDs. These detections have certain requirements on the structure of the target itself and the UV absorption of the target. Therefore, analysis and detection based on these effects have certain limitations in the selection of targets. Thus, it is necessary to develop a strategy that suitable for the selective detection of most targets.

Molecularly imprinted technology (MIT) is one of the attractive strategies to improve the selectivity of QDs-based sensors [18]. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) were the product of MIT, and it is a kind of polymeric material that synthesized through the polymerization between functional monomers and cross-linking, including template molecules (target analytes) [19]. After the target analytes are removed, MIPs with specific shape, size and chemical functionality of the target analytes are obtained. The MIPs only have specific recognition ability for imprinted target analytes. cent MIPs combine the high selectivity of MIPs with the high sensitivity of fluorescence detection, which is considered an attractive tool to selectively identify target analytes [20,21]. Recently, some literature have been reported that several florescent nanosensors based MIPs and QDs for detection of target analytes including CdTe QDs for selective sensing sulfadimidine [22], graphene oxide quantum dots (GO QDs) for the selective detection of entacapone [23] and polymer-coated Mn-doped ZnS QDs for the selective determination of cyphenothrin [24]. Thus, the synthetic strategy of MIPs based on Si QDs is of great feasibility and the selective detection for common targets based on Si QDs is of bright prospects.

Catechol (1,2-dihydroxybenzene, CT), one of vital reagents for industry, is inevitably used in various field, such as flavoring agents, pesticides, dyestuff, medicine and agricultural chemicals [25–27]. Besides, CT is also an important by-product in chemical production. Owing to the high toxicity and low degradability, CT exhibits an extremely larger pollution to the environment [28]. What's worse, the absorption of CT is seriously harmful to humans, it can affect the nerve center system of human beings and even lead to carcinogenicity [29]. Thus, it is very urgent to develop an effective analytical method for the rapid determination of CT. To date, several techniques have been reported for determination of CT, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [30], electrochemical methods [31], gas chromatography (GC) [32], ultraviolet spectrometry [33], etc. However, it cannot be ignored that these methods have limitations because of the sophisticated and expensive instruments or complicated processes. Compared to those methods, fluorescence approach [34] has attracted wide attention due to their low-cost, simplicity, cheap equipment, high sensitivity and fast response [35]. Exactly, CT happens to be a typical target that does not have a special group. It is also impossible for CT to exist in the environment independently of other substances. Therefore, the selective detection of CT based on Si QDs-based fluorescence sensors can be listed as a typical case of Si QDs in the selective identification of common targets without special groups. Thus, it is of great significance and demonstration effect to establish fluorescent measurements for the selective detection of CT based on Si QDs in a complex testing environment.

In the present work, a novel highly selective and sensitive fluorescent nanosensor based on Si QDs (Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs) was introduced for selective detection of CT. In the synthesis process of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs, Si QDs contacted with CT via hydrogen bonding directly, the TEOS served as crossing linker and NH3·H2O was the initiator. The photoluminescence properties and morphologies of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs were completely investigated and characterized. Under the optimal conditions, Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs showed the capability of ultrasensitive and high selective recognition for CT. Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs were further applied to the CT determination in river water. The accuracy and reliability of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs by fluorescence analysis method were finally verified by HPLC method. Thus, the strategy in this work provided a feasible method for the selective detection of CT, even provided a typical example for the selective detection of targets without special groups like CT.

Catechol (CT) (AR, 99%), trisodium citrate dihydrate (SC) (AR, 99%), 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (APTMS) (97%), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) (99.99%), 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) (99%), acetic acid (HPLC, 99.9%), bisphenol a (BPA) (AR, 99%), 2,4-Dichlorophenol (2,4-DP) (AR, 98%), 2,6-Dichlorophenol (2,6-DP) (AR, 99%), 2,4,6-Trichlorophenol (2,4,6-TP) (AR, 98%), resorcinol (RS) (AR, 99%) and hydroquinone (HQ) (AR, 99%) were all purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, Chain). NH3·H2O (AR, 25%−28%), acetonitrile (AR, 99%), ethanol (75%) and methanol (HPLC, 99.8%) were obtained from Guoyao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade reagents, and water used in the whole experiment was deionized water.

The Si QDs were prepared according to previous works with a little adjustment [36]. Typically, SC (1.0 g) was dissolved in deionized water (30 mL) under continuous aeration of N2 for about 30 min. Then 1.0 mL of APTMS was quickly injected into the above solution, following by stirring up to 30 min, and continue to bubble with N2 for 10 min. The mixture was subsequently transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 160 °C for 12 h. After being cooled down, the as-prepared blue fluorescent Si QDs were washed by acetonitrile to dissolve large particles and unreacted reactants. Finally, the Si QDs were continuously 12 h dialyzed (1000 Da, molecular weight cutoff) for further purification. The purified Si QDs were stored at 4.0 °C in the refrigerator for further use.

The Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs was synthesized via a sol-gel copolymerization process. 32.4 mL ethanol, 49.5 mL deionized water, 5.0 mL Si QDs, 11 mg CT (template molecule) and 5.0 mL APTES (functional monomer) were mixed and stirred equably at 1100 rpm in a 500 mL round bottom flask. After stirred for 2.0 h, 91 mL ethanol, 9.0 mL TEOS (cross-linker) and 18 mL NH3·H2O (evocating agent) were added to the mixture. Then the reaction was magnetically stirred at 800 rpm for 24 h at room temperature to obtain the product. At last, the product was collected by centrifugation, washed alternately with deionized water and ethanol for two times and eluted with 100 mL solution of methanol/acetic acid (9:1, v/v) to remove the target CT, then dried at 60 °C for further use. The synthesis of fluorescent non-imprinted polymer (Si QDs@SiO2 @NIPs) was similar to Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs except the addition of CT.

The fluorescent research was conducted under the following procedures. First, moderate Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs (about 200 mg/mL) was dispersed as a stock solution. Then, 500 µL of stock solution was dissolved in 5.0 mL deionized water as test solution, and the CT solution with different concentrations (0–100 µM) gradient were prepared respectively. Therewith, CT solution with a certain concentration were added to test solution to take fluorescent detection. Before performing fluorescence spectral measurements (λEx=360 nm), the mixture was shaken to ensure that they reacted completely. For BPA, RS, HQ and other interfering ions, replaced the CT solution with the corresponding solution, and the fluorescence measurement steps were carried out as described above. The scan speed was 1000 nm·min−1, excitation and emission slit widths were both set at 10 nm, the energy of the photomultiplier tube was set to 700 V, and all the fluorescence detection were tested at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CT in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability of this probe to detect CT in actual water samples (a local river water sample in Jiangsu University) was performed. First of all, these samples were centrifuged and filtered with filter papers to remove large particles. After that, various amounts of CT were measured in actual water samples with the standard addition method and the detection results were recorded.

A HPLC (Agilent 1260 Infinity II) with Wonda Cract ODS-2 reversed-phase column (250 mm × 4.6 mm, 5 µm) were used for chromatographic separation at the room temperature. The mobile phase was an isocratic eluent of the mixture of methanol and 0.2% acetic acid in water (v: v=30%: 70%) under a 277 nm UV detector. The flow rate was set at 0.8 mL·min−1 and the injected volume of samples was 20 µL with a detection time of 18 min. The river water was centrifuged to take the supernatant and remove the precipitates in the samples. Then the 0.22 µm filter was used to filter out macromolecular impurities. Finally, the actual samples were sampling and testing by HPLC analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Si QDs and Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 was the specific synthesis process of Si QDs and Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs. Si QDs with favorable water solubility via a facile hydrothermal method by simply mixing the silicon source APTMS and reductant SC. Then the Si QDs were coated by silica layers via sol-gel polycondensation. The silica layer protected Si QDs from leaking meanwhile retained original photo-luminescence efficiency and avoided being interfered by other matters. Finally, the Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs was prepared by the surface molecular imprinting process. Concretely, Si QDs were firstly modified with APTES, then interacted with the CT via hydrogen bonding. Meanwhile, with the help of the cross-linker TEOS and NH3·H2O, it was able to cause a sol-gel reaction, which a thin imprinting layer was formed. As shown in the illustration in Scheme 1b, the fluorescence intensity of the sensor recovered after the template CT was eluted.

The morphology of Si QDs and Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs was investigated. As shown in Fig. 2a, a vast number of Si QDs were observed, revealing well-separated and uniform dispersion without apparent aggregation. And it could be seen that the average particle size of Si QDs was approximately 3.86 nm (Fig. S1a). TEM and SEM images of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs was intuitively presented in Fig. 2b and c. It displayed that the prepared Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs showed a spherical shape with smooth surface and good monodispersibility. The size distribution of it was 762 nm, according to Fig. S1b. Afterwards, the elemental distribution mapping (Fig. S2) and LSCM (Fig. S3) jointly displayed that the Si QDs was successfully coated into the silica layers, also indicated that Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs contained C, N, O and Si atoms.

The FT-IR spectra of as-prepared Si QDs, Si QDs@SiO2 @NIPs and Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs were showed in Fig. 3 to identify the different surface functional groups. The absorption peaks at 3401 cm−1 and 3138 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibrations of N-H and O-H respectively. The absorption peak at 2933 cm−1 was pertained to C-H unsaturated stretching vibration. And the strong signal at 1578 cm−1 was attributed to the skeleton vibration peak of C-C in the benzene ring. The absorption peak sited at 1389 cm−1 was flexural vibration peak of N-H. The sharp absorbance band at 1079 cm−1 related to the stretching vibration of Si-O-Si. The characteristic peaks at 790 cm−1 and 464 cm−1 revealed Si-O vibration. In general, these results confirmed that there were an abundance of hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of the Si QDs and Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs was successfully developed, which endured them with good water solubility and facilitated their applications as a fluorescent probe in aqueous media. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs was assigned to the silicon (111) plane, revealing that the synthesized Si QDs were in amorphous state, further exhibiting the successfully fabrication of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs, as shown in Fig. 4f.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) plot of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs was collected in Fig. 4 to further elucidate the elemental composition. Four peaks at 102.8 eV, 284.8 eV, 399.05 eV and 532.1 eV, were proved the existence of Si2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 4a). Moreover, the C1s spectrum (Fig. 4b) exhibited four peaks at 284.5 eV, 285.0 eV, 285.6 eV and 286.1 eV, which could be attributed to C-C, C-H, C-N and C-O groups, respectively. Simultaneously, two types of nitrogen atoms N-C and N-H showed by high resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) were respectively assigned to the peaks at 399.1 eV and 401.1 eV. The peaks at 531.2 eV and 532.4 eV in high-resolution O1s (Fig. 4d) spectrum, which exhibited the existence of Si-O, and C-OH, respectively. The high-resolution Si2p (Fig. 4e) spectrum indicated the presence of Si-O group (102.6 eV and 103.2 eV) and Si-C (101.4 eV). In general, XPS analysis results were consistent with the FT-IR data.

Optimized the excitation wavelength of Si QDs to obtain better analytical performance. It was displayed in Fig. S4 that the fluorescence emission spectra of Si QDs at various excitation wavelengths (300–400 nm). It was found that Si QDs owned the optimal emission peak when excited at 360 nm. Furthermore, the optical properties of the as-prepared Si QDs were demonstrated by fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption ( Fig. 5). It was quite clear that the strong absorption peak at 331 nm was attributed to n-π * transition (curve c) [37]. The emission peak of the fluorescent Si QDs appeared at 447 nm (curve b) when the excitation wavelength was at 360 nm (curve a). Meanwhile, under UV irradiation, the synthesized Si QDs emitted the blue fluorescence and presented pellucid solution in the sunlight (seen in the inset of Fig. 5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the detection conditions of Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain better detection results, some important factors including optical stability, pH value and response time were optimized further. First of all, it was necessary to investigate the optical stability before testing. As exhibited in Fig. 6a, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I0/I) was considerably stable for 21 times of tests within 120 min, herein, I0 and I represented for the fluorescence intensity of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs under the conditions of absence and presence of CT, respectively. These results shown that Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs owned favorable stability due to possible the excellent protection of silica layers and could be used for the next fluorescence detection.

Investigation of the pH value on the probe was of great importance. The effect of pH value on Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs ranged from 3.0 to 11 before and after exposing to 80 µM CT was studied, as shown in Fig. 6b. The Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs indicated the most fluorescence intensity decrease at pH 7.0, thus a pH value of 7.0 was chosen in this work.

The influence of response time on the fluorescence intensity ratio (I0/I) calculated at different time scales after the addition of 80 µM CT, which was shown in Fig. 6c. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs occurred an obvious fluorescence quenching for the first 10 min after the CT added, and then it reached stable. Owing to the reaction taken a certain amount of time to complete the process of fluorescence quenching. Therefore, 10 min was adopted in the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the Si QDs@SiO2@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para:
Under optimized conditions, the analytical performance of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs and Si QDs@SiO2 @NIPs with different concentrations from 0 µM to 100 µM was investigated ( Fig. 7). The prepared Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs had an emission at 447 nm. As was demonstrated by Fig. 7a, the fluorescence intensity of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs reduced with the increase of CT concentration in the range of 0–100 µM. The linear equation was I
0
/I= 0.00873 C
CT
+ 0.93385, with a statistically significant correlation coefficient of 0.99303 (R2), where C
CT was the CT concentration (µM), F
0 and F was the fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite before and after the addition of CT, respectively (Fig. 7c). In addition, the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.018 µM was obtained based on a 3δ/s (δ represented the relative standard deviation of blank measurement and s referred to the slope of the calibration curve).

Meanwhile, the dose response of Si QDs@SiO2 @NIPs to CT was examined. As shown in Fig. 7b, the relative fluorescence intensity of Si QDs@SiO2 @NIPs also decreased under different CT concentrations. The concentration of CT was ranged from 0 µM to 100 µM. There was a good linear relationship (Fig. 7d) between the relative fluorescence intensity (I
0
/I) and the concentration of CT (C
CT , µM). The linear equation was I
0
/I = 0.00299 C
CT
+ 1.01819, and correlation coefficient was 0.99018 (R2). The imprinting factor (IF) (IF= s
MIP
/s
NIP ) [38] was calculated to 2.92, which was high than 2.0 for Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs. It was clearly showed that the decrease of fluorescence intensity of the Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs was greater than that of the Si QDs@SiO2 @NIPs by the template molecule CT at the same concentration. This was understandable because the Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs formed special CT binding sites during the process of imprinting, while the non-imprinted Si QDs@SiO2 @NIPs just contained non-special binding sites for CT.

The selectivity of the developed Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs was evaluated by comparing the fluorescence intensity of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs after interaction with CT and other analogs, including BPA, 2, 6-DP, HQ, 2, 4-DP, RS and 2, 4, 6-TP (the corresponding molecular structures were showed in Fig. 8b). As showed in Fig. 8a, the fluorescence intensity of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs showed a strongly higher response to 100 μM CT and a much lower signal for other 100 μM analogs (the blue bars in Fig. 8a). As a comparison, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I0/I) of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs with 100 μM CT and 100 μM analogs (orange bars in Fig. 8a), Si QDs@SiO2 @NIPs with 100 μM targets (green bars in Fig. 8a) and Si QDs@SiO2 @NIPs with 100 μM CT and 100 μM analogs (pink bars in Fig. 8a) were also investigated respectively, revealing the fluorescence intensity of Si QDs@SiO2 @NIPs decreased by targets was much lower than that of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs. The fluorescence intensity of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs and Si QDs@SiO2 @NIPs decreased by 100 μM CT and 100 μM analogs were much lower than that decreased just by 100 μM targets. These results indicated the Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs possessed a superior selectivity for CT detection even in the complex detection environment. This could be attributed to the specific CT recognition sites caused by the preparation process of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs; therefore, CT could be strongly bounded to Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs and lead the fluorescence quenching. While, the Si QDs@SiO2 @NIPs could not effectively bind CT as well as its analogs.

To further explore the anti-interference ability of the Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs in real sample, the effect of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs and Si QDs@SiO2 @NIPs had been studied in the presence of interfering ions, including 5.0 mM Cu2+, Na+, Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Fe3+, NO3 -, Cl- and SO4 2-. As shown in Fig. 9, it could be seen that the coexisting ions induced tiny obvious interference for CT detection. On the contrary, only the addition of CT generated a clearly increase of I0/I at the same dose. The results confirmed that both the Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs and Si QDs@SiO2 @NIPs owned an excellent selectivity of the probe toward CT sensing.

For better exploring the quenching mechanism of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs by CT, a series of characterization were conducted. As showed in Fig. 10a, the black line, red line and blue line were the UV absorption curves of CT, Si QDs and Si QDs with CT respectively, the absorption position of CT in black line and Si QDs in red line have almost no changes compared with that in blue line. It obviously indicated that the quenching mechanism couldn’t be static quenching (SQ). The pink curve and green curve in Fig. 10a were the fluorescence excitation spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum of Si QDs respectively, it could be judged from the two curves and UV absorption that the quenching mechanism couldn’t be fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [39]. Secondly, the zeta potentials of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs and CT were detected and showed in Fig. 10b, the results were − 2.21 mV and + 12.91 mV respectively. The Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs were not a kind of electron-rich substance, therefore, the electron transfer (ET) [40] might not be the main quenching mechanism. Finally, the fluorescence lifetime of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs and Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs with CT were detected and displayed in Fig. 10c and d respectively. After linear fitting [41,42], the τave of Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs and Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs with CT were calculated to 8.585 ns and 3.992 ns respectively (τ0/τ1 ‡ 1), the lifetime of Si QDs had a significant decrease after the addition of CT, which indicated that the inner filter effect (IFE) might not be the possible quenching mechanism [43]. The dynamic quenching process [44] is a process that competes with the spontaneous emission process to shorten the lifetime of excited molecules. The efficiency of dynamic quenching is controlled by the lifetime of the excited molecules of the fluorescent substance and the concentration of the quencher. Moreover, the measured linear equation in the section of “3.3Analytical performance of the Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs” was consistent with a dynamic Stern-Volmer quenching mechanism. Summarize the results above, the main quenching mechanism of Si QDs by CT must be considered to the dynamic quenching.

The practical performance of the Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs for CT detection was studied using standard addition method. Recoveries were detected by spiking the amount of CT in river water samples. After that, the samples were diluted within the linear range and analyzed it according to the above method. Under the optimum conditions, for river water samples, the average recoveries were in the range of 97.77%− 101.70% with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 1.53%− 2.06% for CT ( Table 1). Meanwhile, the CT concentrations in river water were measured via HPLC method, the retention time of CT in HPLC analysis was at 12.596 min and the results were showed in Fig. S5. The comparison of detection results between fluorescence analysis method and HPLC analysis method were showed in Table 1, it could be found that the concentration of CT in river water by HPLC method was detected for three times and calculated to 0.104 µM, 0.197 µM and 0.224 µM respectively. The HPLC detection results were basically in the range of the found concentration via fluorescence analysis method, which greatly verified the accuracy of fluorescence analysis method.

Furthermore, compared with the previous reported sensors for CT detection. As presented in Table 2, the proposed Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs exhibited relatively favorable analytical performances among other sensors in terms of the detection limit. Besides, the linear range of the Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs was also lower than other sensors, which indicated that the Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs was suitable for low concentration detection of CT. The results above reflected that the Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs were feasible and reliable for CT detection in actual river water samples.

In summary, the highly fluorescent water-soluble Si QDs were successfully synthesized by a simple method and the as prepared Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs were deftly used for CT detection in river water samples. Furthermore, the method proposed in this work was proved to be an accurate method via the comparison of HPLC detection results. The Si QDs@SiO2 @MIPs probe combined the advantages of fluorescence detection and MIT, showing the high sensitivity and selectivity to CT, which provides a feasible and reliable method for the analysis concerning Si QDs in complex environment. To summarize, the present work displayed a simple, low-cost, accurate, high selective and sensitive detection method for CT, even for many other targets.

Yeqing Xu: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Ting Huang: Analysis and testing, supplementary data. Suao Wang: Chart optimization, experiment optimization. Minjia Meng: Visualization, Investigation, Supervision, Software, Validation, Writing – reviewing & editing. Yongsheng Yan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21808089), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2019M661750, 2019T120400), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20190245), Major Projects of Education Department of Jiangsu Province (No. 17KJA530003), Programs of Senior Talent Foundation of Jiangsu University (No. 15JDG024), Natural Science Foundation of Jilin Province (No. 20200201102JC).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2021.106850.
